title: Chapter 16: The First Day Free
content: Chloe slept in Elena's guest room until noon.

She woke to sunlight she had not felt in weeks — not filtered through institutional blinds, not broken by security bars. Real sunlight, coming through a real window, onto a real bed that was not a regulation hospital cot.
She lay still for a long moment, just breathing.
Eventually, she forced herself up. The guest room was small but comfortable — a bookshelf, a desk, a window overlooking a quiet street. Normal. Boring. Perfect.
She found Elena in the kitchen, reading a newspaper at the counter. The front page headline: *SERENITY PEAK SCANDAL: Judge Orders Release of All Patients*.
"You made the front page," Elena said without looking up.
Chloe took the seat across from her. "Already?"
"Already. The judge's ruling was big news. Civil rights lawyers are already calling. I had to turn my phone off."
"How many calls?"
"Seventy-three." Elena finally looked up. "And that was just the first hour."

Chloe picked up the newspaper. The article was long — a full investigation piece, complete with quotes from former staff, financial records, and patient testimonials. Mia's hack had worked beyond anything they had imagined.
"What happens now?" she asked.
"Now the legal machine starts. Every patient gets independent counsel. The facility gets investigated. Webber gets a very uncomfortable meeting with the state medical board."
"And my father?"
Elena's expression shifted. "That is the complicated part."
"It always is."
"The documents you provided link Serenity Peak to a shell company owned by Hart, Chen and Associates. Your father's firm. The prosecutors will want to talk to him."
Chloe nodded slowly. She had known this was coming. She had prepared for it. But knowing and feeling were different things.
"Will they arrest him?"
"Not immediately. He will get a chance to cooperate. But if he does not — yes. Eventually."
Chloe stared at the newspaper headline without reading it.
"He put me in here," she said quietly. "He signed the papers. He paid the bills. He knew exactly what this place was."
"I know."
"So why do I feel guilty?"

Elena was quiet for a moment. Then she said: "Because you are a good person. Good people feel guilty when they hurt others, even when the others deserved it. That is not a weakness. It is proof that they did not break you."
Chloe looked down at her hands. They were steady. For the first time in weeks, her hands were steady.
"I want to see them," she said. "Tiffany. Amara. Mia. I want to be there when they walk out."
Elena nodded. "I will drive you."
The Serenity Peak parking lot looked different in daylight.

Not because the building had changed — it was still the same glass-and-stone facade, the same manicured hedges, the same discreet sign that said *WELLNESS CENTER* in elegant script. But the news vans were gone. The police cars were gone. The only vehicle in the lot was a single county transport van, waiting at the side entrance.
Chloe got out of Elena's car and walked toward the gate.
The security guard recognized her. He did not stop her. He just stepped aside, eyes on the ground, and let her through.
Inside, the facility felt hollow.

The hallways were quiet. The staff she passed looked at her with a mix of resentment and fear. One orderly — the same man who had escorted her to Isolation — turned and walked the other way when he saw her coming.
She found them in the common room.

Tiffany was sitting on a couch, a small duffel bag at her feet, staring at the door like she was afraid it would lock before she reached it. Amara was pacing by the window, phone pressed to her ear, arguing with someone in rapid Mandarin. Mia was at the table, laptop open, typing furiously — still working, even now.
Tiffany saw her first.
She stood up. The duffel bag fell off her lap. She crossed the room in five steps and threw her arms around Chloe.
"You did it," Tiffany whispered. Her voice was shaking. "You actually did it."
Chloe hugged her back. Tiffany smelled like the facility's lavender soap — the same smell that had clung to everything for weeks. But soon it would wash off.
"We did it," Chloe said. "All of us."
Amara hung up and turned around. Her face was flushed — from anger or relief, it was hard to tell.
"My father is threatening to disown me," she announced.
"Classic," Chloe said.
"He says I brought shame on the family. That I should have kept my mouth shut and let them handle it privately."
"Let me guess — 'handling it privately' meant a quiet transfer to another facility."
Amara's silence was the answer.
"Well," Chloe said, "good thing you did not keep your mouth shut."
Amara stared at her for a long moment. Then a smile broke through — the first genuine one Chloe had seen from her.
"Yeah," Amara said. "Good thing."

Mia finally looked up from her laptop. "I have been tracking the data traffic. Serenity Peak's parent company is liquidating assets as we speak. They are trying to move money before the courts freeze their accounts."
"Can you stop them?" Chloe asked.
Mia's eyes glittered. "I already flagged three offshore transfers to the SEC. They will not see that money again."
"That is my hacker," Chloe said.
"Former hacker. Current cybersecurity consultant. The job offers started coming in this morning."
"Seriously?"
"A firm in Palo Alto. They saw my name in the news and tracked me down. Said they admire my 'creative approach to network security.'"
Chloe laughed. It was the first real laugh she had let out in weeks.
"What about you?" she asked Tiffany.

Tiffany shrugged. "My parents are flying in from New York. They want to 'discuss things.' I told them I am not going back to their house. I am not going back to their life. I am going to find my own apartment and figure out what I actually want."
"That is huge," Chloe said.
"I know. I am terrified."
"Good. Terror means you are doing it right."
They stood there for a moment — four women in a room that had been designed to break them, now looking at a door that was finally unlocked.
"What about you?" Mia asked Chloe. "What is next?"
Chloe thought about it.
The legal case. Her father. The facility's transformation. Jake, somewhere out there, probably watching the news with growing panic.
"I have a few things to take care of," she said. "But first — I want to see this place burn."
Mia closed her laptop. "The media is already doing that part."
"Then I want to watch."
They walked out together.

Tiffany first, clutching her duffel bag like it contained everything she owned — because it did. Amara next, phone already ringing again, ignoring it. Mia last, laptop tucked under her arm, scanning the parking lot like she was still looking for threats.
Chloe brought up the rear. She paused at the threshold and looked back.
The common room was empty. The hallways were silent. The facility that had held her for thirty-two days was already becoming a ghost.

She thought about the women still inside — the ones who had not been released yet, the ones whose families were still fighting the court order. She thought about the staff who had looked the other way. She thought about Dr. Webber, somewhere in his office, probably on the phone with a lawyer.
Then she thought about the Manifesto.
*Rule 10: The best revenge is living well. But watching them fall is a close second.*
She stepped through the door.
The sunlight hit her face. Warm. Real. Unfiltered.
Behind her, the gate clicked shut.
She did not look back again.
